questions.prompt: From which characteristic curves do you extract h_fe and h_oe?
questions.explanation: h_fe = ΔI_C/ΔI_B at constant V_CE (spacing between curves) and h_oe = ΔI_C/ΔV_CE at constant I_B (slope of one curve) — both are measured from the output characteristics.

options: Spacing between adjacent I_B curves on the output characteristics
Slope of a single I_B curve on the output characteristics at the Q-point
Horizontal distance between input characteristic curves
questions.correctAnswer: Slope of a single I_B curve on the output characteristics at the Q-point
questions.explanation: h_oe = ΔI_C/ΔV_CE at constant I_B. This is literally the slope (steepness) of one output characteristic curve at the Q-point. A perfectly flat curve would give h_oe = 0 (infinite output resistance).

questions.prompt: If the output curves of a BJT were perfectly horizontal and evenly spaced, what would be true?
questions.explanation: Horizontal curves mean zero slope → h_oe = 0 (infinite output resistance). Evenly spaced means ΔI_C/ΔI_B is the same everywhere → h_fe is constant regardless of Q-point.

questions.prompt: On the input characteristics, h_ie is:
options: The spacing between two V_CE curves at constant I_B
The slope ΔV_BE/ΔI_B measured on one V_CE curve at the Q-point
questions.correctAnswer: The slope ΔV_BE/ΔI_B measured on one V_CE curve at the Q-point
questions.explanation: h_ie is the input impedance — the ratio of input voltage change to input current change. On the V_BE vs I_B plot, this is the slope of one specific V_CE curve at the Q-point. Typical value: ~1 kΩ for CE.

questions.prompt: The typical input impedance of a common base configuration is about:
questions.explanation: CB has a very low input impedance (~22 Ω) because the input is at the emitter, which presents a resistance of approximately V_T/I_EQ. This contrasts sharply with CE (~1 kΩ) which has β times higher input impedance.

questions.prompt: Common base has a very high output resistance (≈ 2 MΩ). This makes it ideal for:
questions.explanation: A high output resistance means the output current stays nearly constant regardless of the load — the defining property of a good current source. CB's h_ob ≈ 0.49 μA/V gives 1/h_ob ≈ 2 MΩ.

questions.prompt: Why does h_fe change when you move the Q-point to a different location?
options: Because the curves are exactly parallel
Because the spacing between curves varies across the graph due to nonlinearity
h_fe never changes — it is truly constant
questions.correctAnswer: Because the spacing between curves varies across the graph due to nonlinearity
questions.explanation: The output characteristic curves are not perfectly uniform — spacings between IB curves vary. At different V_CE or I_B values, the ratio ΔI_C/ΔI_B changes. This is a direct consequence of the BJT's nonlinear nature.

questions.prompt: If h_oe = 25 μA/V for a CE BJT, the output resistance is:
questions.explanation: Output resistance = 1/h_oe = 1/(25×10⁻⁶) = 40,000 Ω = 40 kΩ. Remember: h_oe is a conductance (siemens), so invert to get resistance (ohms).

questions.prompt: Which factor can cause h-parameter values to change even at the same Q-point?
questions.explanation: Temperature significantly affects h-parameters. For example, h_fe (β) typically increases with temperature because the intrinsic carrier concentration and diffusion coefficients are temperature-dependent. This is why thermal stability is important in BJT circuit design.

questions.prompt: h_re is almost always negligible (≈ 10⁻⁴) because:
options: Changes in V_CE have virtually no effect on V_BE for CE and CB configurations
The input characteristics are identical for all V_CE values
The output resistance is too high
questions.correctAnswer: Changes in V_CE have virtually no effect on V_BE for CE and CB configurations
questions.explanation: h_re = ΔV_BE/ΔV_CE. On the input characteristics, the curves for different V_CE values are almost identical — changing V_CE by several volts shifts V_BE by only fractions of a millivolt. This makes h_re ≈ 10⁻⁴, negligible in most analyses.
